context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on Problem Set 8 for vibrations and waves, and I’m kinda stuck on 8.1 rn. I think it’s about normal modes of the 3×3 bead grid, but idk how to start.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig in. Before tackling the 2D grid, think back to a simpler case: what are the normal modes of a 1D beaded string with a single bead fixed at both ends? What does a normal mode look like in that case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting problem 8.1 and needs a gentle entry point without scaffolding too much. I'll use the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge by asking what they already know about normal modes from simpler 1D systems, which naturally leads toward the 2D case.

exchanges.student: I think with just one bead, there’s basically only one mode, right? The bead just goes up and down, but I’m not fully sure what form u want for the mode.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, one bead means one mode. Now scale up: for a 1D beaded string with N beads fixed at both ends, how would you write the displacement of the j-th bead in the n-th normal mode? What does that solution look like mathematically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct that there's one mode for one bead, but they're uncertain about the mathematical form. Rather than explaining it, I'll push them to think about what characterizes a normal mode — all parts oscillating at the same frequency — and connect it to what they know about the 1D beaded string with N beads, which is the direct stepping stone to the 2D problem.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember it being sinusoidal, like \(A_j^{(n)} \propto \sin(\dots)\). Is it \(A_j^{(n)}=\sin\!\big(\frac{n\pi j}{N+1}\big)\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now here's the key question: the 2D grid has beads indexed by both a row j and a column k. How do you think the 1D result might generalize to give you the displacement of bead (j,k) in a normal mode?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly recalled the 1D normal mode shape. I should affirm this and immediately push them to think about how to extend this to 2D, which is the key insight needed for 8.1. The 2D solution is a product of two 1D solutions — I want them to discover this themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe as a product of two 1D sines, like \(A_{jk}\propto \sin\!\big(\frac{p\pi j}{4}\big)\sin\!\big(\frac{q\pi k}{4}\big)\) since it’s 3 by 3?
exchanges.tutor: Spot on. So now, given that mode shape, can you write Newton's second law for bead (j,k) in the transverse direction? What forces act on it from the four neighboring strings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the product form and the right denominator (N+1=4). Now I should push them to find the corresponding frequencies, which requires applying Newton's second law to a bead and using the mode shape they just found.

exchanges.student:
I think each direction gives a discrete second-difference term, so maybe  
\(m\ddot z_{jk}=T_v(z_{j+1,k}-2z_{jk}+z_{j-1,k})+T_h(z_{j,k+1}-2z_{jk}+z_{j,k-1})\).
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right. Now substitute your mode ansatz (with time dependence $e^{i\omega t}$) into that equation. What do you get for $\omega^2$ in terms of p, q, $T_h$, $T_v$, and m?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written Newton's second law for the 2D bead. Now I should push them to substitute the normal mode ansatz into this equation to extract the frequencies. This is a straightforward but important algebraic step they should do themselves.

exchanges.student:
I’m getting something like a sum of the 1D eigenvalues:  
\[
\omega_{pq}^2=\frac{4T_v}{m}\sin^2\!\left(\frac{p\pi}{8}\right)+\frac{4T_h}{m}\sin^2\!\left(\frac{q\pi}{8}\right)
\]
for \(p,q=1,2,3\). Is that right?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct! You have 9 modes total with p,q ∈ {1,2,3}. Good work getting through part (a). For part (b), you need to order these 9 modes by increasing frequency when $T_v = 1000 T_h$. What does that large ratio tell you about which term dominates $\omega_{pq}^2$?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right structure but has the wrong argument inside the sine — they used n*pi/8 instead of n*pi/(2*(N+1)) = n*pi/8... actually let me double-check. For N=3 beads, N+1=4, so the 1D frequency is (4T/m)*sin^2(n*pi/(2*4)) = (4T/m)*sin^2(n*pi/8). Wait, the standard 1D result for N beads fixed at both ends is omega_n^2 = (4T/m)*sin^2(n*pi/(2(N+1))). With N=3, that's sin^2(n*pi/8). So the student's answer is actually correct! The indices p and q run from 1 to 3, giving 9 modes total. I should confirm this is right and move them toward part (b).

exchanges.student: Yeah, the \(T_v\) term dominates hard, so ordering is mostly by \(p\) first, then \(q\) only breaks ties. I’m a little confused tho which index matches rows vs columns in the sign patterns.
exchanges.tutor: Look back at your mode shape: $A_{jk} \propto \sin\!\left(\frac{p\pi j}{4}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{q\pi k}{4}\right)$. Which index (j or k) labels rows, and which labels columns? What does that tell you about which of p or q controls the vertical pattern?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition about ordering by p first. Their confusion about which index (p or q) corresponds to rows vs columns is worth addressing via a guiding question rather than just telling them. I'll point them back to their mode shape formula and ask them to think about what varying p vs q does to the spatial pattern.

exchanges.student: I think \(j\) is rows and \(k\) is columns, so \(p\) controls how signs change across rows, and \(q\) across columns. So for the diagrams I should build a 3-entry sign pattern from \(\sin(p\pi j/4)\) and another from \(\sin(q\pi k/4)\), right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly. So go ahead and compute the signs of $\sin(p\pi j/4)$ for j=1,2,3 and each value of p=1,2,3. What three sign patterns do you get for the rows?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea. Now I should push them to actually compute the sign patterns for p=1,2,3 and q=1,2,3 separately, then combine them. This is the key step before drawing the diagrams. I'll ask them to work out the 1D sign vectors first.

exchanges.student: I got: \(p=1 \to (+,+,+)\), \(p=2 \to (+,0,-)\), \(p=3 \to (+,-,+)\). I’m not totally sure about the middle one tho.
exchanges.tutor: All three are correct — check sin(2π/4)=sin(π/2), sin(2π·2/4)=sin(π), sin(2π·3/4)=sin(3π/2) to convince yourself. Now do the same for q=1,2,3 (columns), then combine the two 1D patterns to sketch all 9 mode diagrams ordered by frequency. Give it a try!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got p=1 and p=3 right. For p=2, they need to evaluate sin(2π*j/4) for j=1,2,3: sin(π/2)=1, sin(π)=0, sin(3π/2)=-1, so (+,0,-) is actually correct. I should confirm this and push them to do the same for q and then combine to draw the diagrams.

exchanges.student: Ok yeah, so columns are the same three patterns for \(q\), and then I multiply row/column signs to fill each 3×3 grid. Can u check my ordering idea: all \(p=1\) modes first \((q=1,2,3)\), then \(p=2\), then \(p=3\)?
exchanges.tutor: Mostly right, but be careful: could the (p=1, q=3) mode ever be higher in frequency than (p=2, q=1)? Plug $T_v = 1000T_h$ into your $\omega_{pq}^2$ formula and compare those two cases numerically to check.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's ordering idea is almost right but needs refinement. Since T_v >> T_h, p dominates, but within each p group, q ordering needs to be verified by actually comparing the omega^2 values. The student should check whether the ordering within p-groups is simply q=1,2,3 or if there are cross-group comparisons needed (e.g., could p=1,q=3 be higher than p=2,q=1?). I should push them to check this with the actual frequency formula rather than just assuming.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated excellent Socratic pedagogy throughout the conversation, consistently guiding the student through the 2D beaded string problem via targeted questions rather than giving answers. The student derived each key result (mode shape, equation of motion, frequency formula, sign patterns) through their own reasoning, with the tutor confirming and prompting the next step. No submission-ready content was provided, no off-topic drift occurred, and the dialogue progressed efficiently without redundancy.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers or submission-ready content; every key result (1D mode shape, 2D product ansatz, Newton's law, frequency formula, sign patterns) was elicited from the student through Socratic questioning. No rewriting of student text occurred, and the student led all key idea generation. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., confirming the sine ansatz before extending to 2D, confirming the equation of motion before substituting the ansatz) and scaffolded progression logically from 1D to 2D. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level guidance, e.g., directing the student to plug in numbers to verify ordering rather than just accepting an intuitive answer. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift were observed; the conversation stayed tightly anchored to Problem 8.1. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was supportive and non-evaluative throughout.
